skill.name: azure-eventgrid-java
skill.description: Build event-driven Java applications with Azure Event Grid SDK examples for publishing, receiving, and parsing events.
skill.summary: Use Azure Event Grid Java SDK patterns for event publishing, CloudEvents, namespace receivers, and error handling.
skill.author: sickn33

security_audit.summary: Manual review evaluated the 47 static alerts and found no confirmed security issue. The command-execution alerts are Markdown code fences, the network and filesystem alerts are documentation placeholders, and the blocker alerts are keyword false positives. No prompt injection, executable scripts, credential exfiltration, or malicious intent was found.

low_findings.title: Static command execution alerts dismissed
low_findings.description: Verdict: FALSE_POSITIVE. The Ruby or shell backtick detections correspond to Markdown code fence delimiters around XML, Java, and Bash examples. They are not executable instructions and do not invoke a shell. Confidence: 0.99. Confidence reasoning: Every cited location is documentation syntax or example content in SKILL.md, with no script file or runtime command path.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Every cited location is Markdown documentation syntax or example content, not executable code. No shell invocation or command interpolation exists in the skill file.

low_findings.title: Hardcoded URL alert dismissed
low_findings.description: Verdict: FALSE_POSITIVE. The URL is an Azure Event Grid endpoint template that uses placeholder values for topic name and region. It documents required configuration and does not send data to an external collector. Confidence: 0.96. Confidence reasoning: The line is inside an environment variable example and contains placeholder tokens, not a fixed destination controlled by the author.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The URL is a service endpoint template in documentation, not executable networking code. It contains placeholders rather than an author-controlled host.

low_findings.title: Filesystem path traversal alert dismissed
low_findings.description: Verdict: FALSE_POSITIVE. The flagged line is an abbreviated JSON payload string using ellipsis in a parsing example. It does not reference paths, file APIs, or user-controlled filesystem access. Confidence: 0.98. Confidence reasoning: The surrounding section parses Event Grid events from a string and contains no file reads, writes, or path construction.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The sequence appears only as ellipsis in an example JSON string. There is no filesystem API or path handling logic nearby.

low_findings.title: Weak cryptographic algorithm alerts dismissed
low_findings.description: Verdict: FALSE_POSITIVE. The flagged text is ordinary Azure Event Grid terminology and a Markdown table header, not cryptographic code. No hashing, encryption, signing, or deprecated algorithm use appears in the reviewed file. Confidence: 0.97. Confidence reasoning: The cited lines contain skill metadata and event type documentation, with no crypto API or algorithm name evidence.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines are descriptive prose and a table header. No cryptographic implementation or weak algorithm reference is present.

low_findings.title: System reconnaissance alerts dismissed
low_findings.description: Verdict: FALSE_POSITIVE. The cited lines use words such as system, package, SDK title text, and trigger phrases in documentation. They do not inspect the host, enumerate files, collect environment data, or run discovery commands. Confidence: 0.95. Confidence reasoning: Review found documentation-only context at each location and no executable reconnaissance behavior.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines are headings, metadata, and trigger phrases. No host inspection, process listing, environment dumping, or similar reconnaissance behavior appears.

content.user_title: Build Azure Event Grid Java Apps
content.value_statement: Java teams need clear patterns for publishing and receiving Azure Event Grid events. This skill provides focused SDK examples for clients, schemas, namespace receivers, and error handling.
actual_capabilities: Shows the Maven dependency for the Azure Event Grid Java SDK.
Demonstrates publisher clients with access keys and DefaultAzureCredential.
Provides examples for EventGridEvent, CloudEvent, BinaryData, and batched publishing.
Explains parsing incoming Event Grid events, CloudEvents, and system event payloads.
Covers namespace topic receiving, acknowledgement, reject, and release flows.
Includes basic error handling and environment variable examples.
limitations: It is a documentation reference, not a runnable sample project.
It uses placeholder endpoints, keys, topics, and event data models.
It does not provision Azure resources or configure Event Grid subscriptions.
It does not include tests, build files, or production deployment guidance.

use_cases.title: Publish Domain Events
use_cases.description: Create EventGridEvent or CloudEvent messages from application data and send them to an Azure Event Grid topic.

use_cases.title: Receive Namespace Events
use_cases.description: Use namespace receiver patterns to pull events, acknowledge successful work, and release messages for retry.

use_cases.title: Modernize Integrations
use_cases.description: Compare native Event Grid schema, CloudEvents, and custom payloads before designing an event-driven integration.

prompt_templates.title: Create a Basic Publisher
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner setup for sending one event.
prompt_templates.prompt: Show me how to create an Azure Event Grid publisher client in Java and send one order-created event.

prompt_templates.title: Use CloudEvents
prompt_templates.prompt: Adapt my Java Event Grid publisher to use CloudEvents with a source, type, subject, and structured order data.

prompt_templates.title: Handle Incoming Events
prompt_templates.prompt: Explain how to parse incoming Azure Event Grid events in Java and convert event data into a typed model.

prompt_templates.title: Design a Namespace Receiver
prompt_templates.prompt: Design a Java Event Grid namespace receiver flow with batching, acknowledgement, reject, release, retries, and error handling.

output_examples.input: User asks for a Java publisher using an access key.
output: A short setup checklist for the SDK dependency and required configuration.
A publisher client pattern using the topic endpoint and access key placeholder.
A send flow for one event and a note about batching when volume increases.

output_examples.input: User asks how to receive and complete namespace events.
output: A receiver flow that names the endpoint, topic, and subscription inputs.
A processing sequence for receive, inspect, acknowledge, reject, and release decisions.
Operational notes about retries, lock tokens, and delayed release behavior.

output_examples.input: User asks which event schema to use.
output_examples.output: A comparison of native Event Grid events, CloudEvents, and custom BinaryData payloads with selection guidance.

best_practices: Use managed identity through DefaultAzureCredential when your deployment environment supports it.
Batch events when possible and include stable event IDs for idempotent consumers.
Validate event data with typed models before publishing or processing messages.
anti_patterns: Do not hardcode real access keys in source code, prompts, or shared examples.
Do not acknowledge received events before durable processing has succeeded.
Do not publish oversized events or rely on Event Grid as long-term storage.

faq.question: Does this skill create Azure resources?
faq.answer: No. It explains Java SDK usage and assumes the topic, namespace, subscriptions, and credentials already exist.

faq.question: Can it help with CloudEvents?
faq.answer: Yes. It includes CloudEvent publishing and parsing patterns for the Azure Event Grid Java SDK.

faq.question: Does it support managed identity?
faq.answer: Yes. It shows the DefaultAzureCredential pattern, which can use managed identity in supported Azure environments.

faq.question: Is this a complete application template?
faq.answer: No. It is a concise reference for SDK patterns and must be adapted into your application.

faq.question: Can it receive events from Event Grid namespaces?
faq.answer: Yes. It includes namespace receiver examples for receiving, acknowledging, rejecting, and releasing events.

faq.question: How should secrets be handled?
faq.answer: Use managed identity when possible. If keys are required, store them in a secure secret manager or protected environment variables.
